rationale: The page contains detailed information on three significant topics: the Central Adoption Resource Authority (CARA), the regulatory framework for online content regulation (specifically regarding obscenity), and the Global Gender Gap Report 2025. These sections provide specific legal provisions, institutional roles, and international rankings suitable for generating exam questions.
cleanedText:
Central Adoption Resource Authority (CARA)
- Status: Statutory body under Ministry of Women and Child Development
- Function: Monitors domestic and international adoptions
- Role: Central Authority for inter-country adoptions under Hague Convention (1993), ratified by India in 2003
8.9. ONLINE CONTENT REGULATION
Why in the News?
Ministry of Information and Broadcasting (I&B) has banned public access to several OTT platforms, for streaming obscene content.
More on the news
- They were banned under Information Technology Act, 2000, and Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021.
- Supreme Court in Ranjit D. Udeshi v. State of Maharashtra (1965) defined obscenity as a reasonable restriction to free speech (Article 19).
Regulatory framework for regulation of obscene content
- IT Act 2000
- Section 67 and Section 67A: Deal with publication and transmission of obscene content in electronic form
- Section 69A: Empowers Central Government to direct blocking of public access to information in specific grounds.
- Section 79: Grants “safe harbour” protection to intermediaries (including OTT platforms) from liability for third-party content. However, they could lose protection if they fail to remove unlawful content upon receiving notification from government.
- IT rules 2021 under IT Act, 2000 issued by Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (Meity): Places digital news media and OTT platforms under Ministry of information and broadcasting ministry (MIB).
- Section 294 of Bhartiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2023: Defines the term obscene, criminalizes the dissemination of material including those in electronic form.
- Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POSCO) Act: Made sale and distribution of child pornography illegal.
- Indecent Representation of Women (Prohibition) Act, 1986: Prohibits publication and distribution of materials containing indecent representations of women.
8.10. GLOBAL GENDER GAP, 2025
Why in the News?
Recently, Global Gender Gap Report 2025 was released by World Economic Forum.
Key Findings of Report
- India:
- Ranked 131st out of 148 countries slipping from 129th position in 2024, although score improved to 0.644 (2025) from 0.641 (2024).
- Bangladesh emerged as best performer in South Asia. Nepal, Sri Lanka, Bhutan ranked better than India.
- Global:
- Iceland leads the rankings for the 16th year running, followed by Finland, Norway, the United Kingdom and New Zealand.
